entry.title: The Atlantic: 19 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • U.S. Messaging on Monkeypox Is Deeply Flawed
  • There Is a Planet With Clouds Made of Sand
  • The White Liberals’ Burden
  • Photos: The U.K. Reaches Its Highest Temperature Ever

  • The Supreme Court’s Extreme Power Grab
  • The Cause of the Crime Wave Is Hiding in Plain Sight
  • The Fate of States’ Rights After Roe
  • 20 Reader Ideas for Who Could Replace Biden
  • Wildfires Rage Across Southwestern Europe
  • Do Animals Dream?
  • The War in Ukraine Is Dividing Lifelong Friends
  • ‘France Has Delivered Almost Nothing’
  • The Power Imbalance Undermining Global Health
  • The Everything-Is-Weird Economy
  • The Case for Dating a Friend
  • New COVID Vaccines Will Be Ready This Fall. America Won’t Be.
  • Party Politics
  • What Are Abortion Code Words Even For?
  • Why We Remember Floods and Forget Droughts
  • How to Fix the Bias Against Free Speech on Campus

  • You’ve Never Seen Anything Quite Like The Rehearsal

  • It’s Joe Manchin’s America
  • Mother Nature Dissents
  • The Bannon Strategy
  • The Incitement Paper Trail
  • Netflix’s Persuasion Tries to Have It All
  • ‘Netflix Thinks Exactly Like an Old Movie Studio’

  • TV’s Best New Show Is a Study of Masculinity in Crisis
  • Where the Crawdads Sing Author Wanted for Questioning in Murder
  • The Cult Classic That Captures the Grind of Dead-End Jobs
  • The Older Woman Comes of Age
  • Where to Turn When You Feel ‘at Odds With Being Human’

  • Photos of the Week: Elephant Rescue, Buck Moon, Love Parade
  • Selections From the Audubon Photography Awards Top 100

  • How China Wants to Replace the U.S. Order
  • Everything About Twitter v. Musk Is Utter Nonsense
  • The BA.5 Wave Is What COVID Normal Looks Like

  • Seriously, What’s Making All These Mysterious Space Signals?
  • How to Successfully Smash Your Face Against a Tree

  • Whatever This Is, It Won’t Be Build Back Better
  • The Shame of the Secret Service
  • The Ways We Make a Living
  • Local Prosecutors Can’t Protect Abortion Rights
  • The Hawley-Bridges Exchange Was Not Constructive
  • Trump Terrified Even His Truest Believers
  • Where Is the National Outrage Over Uvalde?
News and editorial about politics, culture, entertainment, tech, etc. Contains many articles not seen in The Atlantic magazine

entry.title: Nautilus: 19 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Land of Extremes: Poetry of the Sonoran Desert
  • Feeling Stressed? Read a Poem
  • This Planetary Scientist Is Always Reaching for Something Big
  • Gravitational Waves Continue to Astound
  • The Case for Popularizing Ocean Science
  • A Surprising Side of Carl Sagan
  • How Do We Get People Who Believe in Pseudoscience to Trust Science?
  • Swimming in Noise
  • The High Price of Cheap Shrimp
  • How Are the Bees?
  • Reshuffled Rivers Bolster the Amazon’s Hyper-Biodiversity
  • Who Are the Scientists Here?
  • The Giraffe Neck Evolved for Sexual Combat
  • Print Edition 43
  • Print Edition 42
  • Print Edition 41
  • Print Edition 40
  • Embryo Cells Set Patterns for Growth by Pushing and Pulling
  • Life Helps Make Almost Half of All Minerals on Earth
  • Under Anesthesia, Where Do Our Minds Go?
  • Thinking Like a Scientist Will Make You Happier
  • Don’t Give Up on Facts
  • Are You a Naïve Realist?
  • Moving Beyond Mimicry in Artificial Intelligence
  • Overthrowing the Patriarchy Through Ecstatic Sex
  • What Oceanographers Can Learn From Their Animal Colleagues
Nautilus is a different kind of science magazine. Our stories take you into the depths of science and spotlight its ripples in our lives and cultures. We believe any subject in science, no matter how complex, can be explained with clarity and vitality.

The Diplomat is a current-affairs magazine for the Asia-Pacific, with news and analysis on politics, security, business, technology and life across the region.

ProPublica is an independent, nonprofit newsroom that produces investigative journalism with moral force.

  • A Grand Unified Theory of Buying Stuff
  • Can Being Reminded of My Death Improve My Life?
Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.

  • Core control principles of the eukaryotic cell cycle
  • De novo design of discrete, stable 310-helix peptide assemblies
  • Structure of the Dicer-2–R2D2 heterodimer bound to a small RNA duplex
  • Structural insights into dsRNA processing by Drosophila Dicer-2–Loqs-PD
  • Restoration prioritization must be informed by marginalized people
  • Reply to: Restoration prioritization must be informed by marginalized people
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

entry.title: Scientific American: July 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Record-Breaking Voyager Spacecraft Begin to Power Down
  • Subverting Climate Science in the Classroom
  • How Parents' Trauma Leaves Biological Traces in Children
  • Toxic Slime Contributed to Earth's Worst Mass Extinction--And It's Making a Comeback
  • Astronomers Gear Up to Grapple with the High-Tension Cosmos
  • 'Momentum Computing' Pushes Technology's Thermodynamic Limits
  • Students Deserve to Learn about the Climate Emergency
  • Readers Respond to the March 2022 Issue
  • How Connected Cars Can Map Urban Heat Islands
  • Marker Tip--Without Ink!--Makes a Hardy Medical Sampler
  • New Satellite Is a 'Swiss Army Knife' in Space
  • Dinosaur Diets May Help Explain Dramatic Diversity
  • How Antarctic Krill Coordinate the Biggest Swarms in the World
  • These Spiders Spring Off Their Mates to Avoid Sexual Cannibalism
  • 'Frog Skin' Cell Type Found in Mammal Mouths
  • News Briefs from around the World: July 2022
  • Venomous Snail Unlocks New Diabetes Drugs
  • See the Strange Underground Detector Probing Neutrino Mysteries
  • Skies Are Sucking More Water from the Land
  • How Snakes Breathe while Crushing Prey
  • Crowdfunding Isn't Enough in a Crisis
  • Why Do Mental Illnesses--From Depression to Schizophrenia--Raise the Risk of Dementia?
  • We Need to Make 'Electrifying Everything' Easier
  • Climate Destroyers Go to Jail, Martian Travel Guide, Bee Interiority, and More
  • 50, 100 & 150 Years Ago: July 2022
  • Wildfires Followed by Severe Rain Will Become More Common
  • Poem: 'On Visible Light'
  • Why Kids Are Afraid to Ask for Help
  • Science Needs to Shrink Its Carbon Footprint
Popular Science. Monthly magazine. Should be downloaded around the middle of each month.

entry.title: Harvard Business Review: 19 Jul, 2022
entry.summary: Harvard Business Review: 19 Jul, 2022 published at 2022-07-01 12:00PM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • A Role That’s More Challenging Than Ever
  • A Seat at the Table Is Not Enough
  • A Common Parasite Can Make People More Entrepreneurial
  • Two Cofounders of Xendit on Pioneering Fintech in Southeast Asia
  • The C-Suite Skills That Matter Most
  • Is It Time to Consider Co-CEOs?
  • When Hiring CEOs, Focus on Character
  • As the World Shifts, So Should Leaders
  • Identifying Unmet Needs in a Digital Age
  • To Drive Diversity Efforts, Don’t Tiptoe Around Your Legal Risk
  • Private Equity Should Take the Lead in Sustainability
  • What You’re Getting Wrong About Customer Journeys
  • A Better Way to Put Your Data to Work
  • Leadership in a Politically Charged Age
  • Why You Need an AI Ethics Committee
  • How to Turn a Supply Chain Platform into an Innovation Engine
  • How to Overcome Your Fear of the Unknown
  • Case Study: Would Vegan Offerings Dilute Our Brand?
  • Exploring the Metaverse
  • Life’s Work: An Interview with Misty Copeland
Harvard Business Review is the leading destination for smart management thinking. Through its flagship magazine, books, and digital content and tools published on HBR.org, Harvard Business Review aims to provide professionals around the world with rigorous insights and best practices to help lead themselves and their organizations more effectively and to make a positive impact.

entry.title: Poetry: July/August 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Dear Reader,
  • we acknowledge ourselves
  • Hasísná
  • Emergence
  • hastiin k’aalógii ’ání
  • butterfly man tells a story
  • 1855
  • That you would be made of rock
  • sound installation
  • Did You Walk to Oklahoma?
  • Katuq.1
  • Examining the Phytolith
  • 𐓨𐓘͘ ́ 𐓺𐓟 𐓱𐓘́𐓱𐓘𐓺𐓟/Máze Htáhtaze/Typewriter
  • 𐓷𐓘𐓧𐓟́𐓺𐓟/Waléze/Stationery
  • Upon My Return to Appalachia
  • Cada día más cerca del fin del mundo
  • Shch’a-wa
  • Influx—Reservation Lockdown
  • Creation
  • Grocery Story
  • Echolocations
  • Red Stick Noonshine
  • Love Shack, Anadarko
  • Lost Jewels
  • Finding Home
  • in new countries more eloquent than elsewhere
  • nothing as beautiful as gravity in the eternal folds
  • 34°54’15’’ N 111°47’30’’ W
  • Shipwreck Pastoral
  • Hotbeds in Norfolk, Virginia
  • Said the Tobacco to the Hand
  • The Shore of This Day
  • Floaters
  • Papyrus Pantoum
  • sáanii kǫ’ yee da’ałt’eis tł’óo’gi
  • Women are cooking outside over the fire
  • Grandma’s Challenge
  • In the City of Eve
  • Cousin Wolf Sings
  • same word in chickasaw for wolf and coyote
  • From the Roof Deck
  • maghrib
  • midnight
  • Learning about Treaties in School
  • Soft Thunder
  • Emerging
  • The Meadow Views: Sword and Symbolic History
  • Bead Workers
  • Bird Killer
  • Northbound #23 Bus (Old Indian Trail #2)
  • Endangered Ghazal with Telegram and Natural World
  • A White Man in the Audience Said I Owed Him
  • Wavelength // Waveless
  • Interrogation in a Nail Salon
  • Of My Body
  • 杨柳枝词
  • The Song of the Willow Branches
  • Field Notes
  • before the dark
  • Well-Traveled Path: An Interview with Arthur Sze
Founded in Chicago by Harriet Monroe in 1912, Poetry is the oldest monthly devoted to verse in the English-speaking world.
